Instead of patching the Producer and Consumer classes can we patch the methods directly. Patching classes leads to some edge cases. It has bit us before with the TracedMongoClient.

If a user imports `confluent_kafka.Producer`  and/or `confluent_kafka.Consumer` then calls ddtrace.patch(kafka=True). We won't patch any Producer or Consumer classes that have already been imported.

Producer is not patched:
```
>>> from confluent_kafka import Producer, Consumer
>>> from ddtrace import patch
>>> patch(kafka=True)
>>> pd = Producer(dict())
%5|1679066975.054|CONFWARN|rdkafka#producer-1| [thrd:app]: No `bootstrap.servers` configured: client will not be able to connect to Kafka cluster
>>> type(pd)
<class 'cimpl.Producer'>
```

Producer is patched:
```
 >>> from ddtrace import patch
>>> patch(kafka=True)
>>> from confluent_kafka import Producer, Consumer
>>> pd = Producer(dict())
%5|1679066848.217|CONFWARN|rdkafka#producer-1| [thrd:app]: No `bootstrap.servers` configured: client will not be able to connect to Kafka cluster
>>> type(pd)
<class 'ddtrace.contrib.kafka.patch.TracedProducer'>
```

As I mentioned in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4970#pullrequestreview-937407074

1. there are many places where LightGBM's C++ code expects 0-based indexes
2. because R is 1-based, there have been some places where the R package has translated from 1-based inputs to 0-based values
3. this has been done very inconsistently
4. this requires careful thought and a comprehensive plan

For example.... should 0-based indices be converted back to 1-based in `Booster$save_model()`? Or is it expected that the model file produced by R code could be directly read by Python code?

What about in `params`? Is it expected that you could save a dictionary from Python code to a file like `params.json`, then read it in R and immediately pass it to `lgb.train(..., params)`?

It's not obvious to me which choice in these and other situations is "right" or "wrong", and I think that deserves a comprehensive plan with guidelines for when users should expect to see 0-based vs. 1-based values. I'd like to do that work at some point, but it is really difficult and a large amount of effort right.

For the purpose of this PR, I recommend just focusing on preserving the current behavior.

First, we make `available_resource_set` a pointer to avoid copy overhead in most cases (there is no normal task and we would not have to get into the `if` branch).

Second, if we get into the `if` branch, we may define `new_available_resource_set` there and make `available_resource_set` point to it. But after the `if` branch, the `new_available_resource_set` would be destructed (because its lifetime is within the `if` scope) and `available_resource_set` will point to a un-initialized space. So we define the `new_available_resource_set` before of the `if` scope.

These are some trivial details, and I'm not sure if it can be added there as comments.

The main reason is actually that we install these requirements before we install ray. Thus if we used `ray[all]` here, we would download the latest ray release, install it, then later compile ray. We've ran into problems with that before...

I'm also not sure if the legacy pip resolver will obey the constraints in `ray[all]` in the same way it obeys constraints in `-c requirements.txt`. The legacy resolver installs all packages as defined on their first level (so `ray` and everything in the provided files), but for second level dependencies (and I think `ray[all]`'s dependencies would be that) it installs the latest versions and accepts incompatibilities.

We want to make sure that Ray's constraints are closely followed. Thus I would like to keep it for now

The Ray Serve REST API server isn't in the dashboard anymore. As part of the HA effort, the server got moved to the dashboard agent. One copy of the agent runs on each node in the Ray cluster. The agent is initialized lazily, so when a node receives a REST request for the first time, it initializes the agent.

So I noted that `ActorClass.__init__` will anyway raise a `TypeError` whenever it will be inherited. To exactly figure out whether the exception is due to inheritance of `ActorClass`, I created a new class `ActorClassInheritanceException(TypeError)`. Now, whenever this will be raised, then `DerivedActorClass` will get a clear signal about inheritance of `ActorClass`. In other cases, it will be safe to conclude (AFAICT) that user called `__init__` method of their class and we will proceed normally. IMHO, this is a better and more robust solution which just depends on a simple signal i.e., raising a particular exception in a specific event. It doesn't matter how inheritance is prevented as in the end we just need to raise `ActorClassInheritanceException` and all other code will be able to detect that easily.

> ... Might be clearer to have the 2 perms checks in an if / else instead of this assumption/precedence set up...

My thought was that having an imperative statement:
```python
if need_for_perm:
  assert_permission(graphene_info, "permission_for_this_condition")
  perform_action()
```
is less visually obvious that a permissions check is happening, whereas a guard structure makes the check more clear:
```python
if need_for_perm and assert_permission(graphene_info, "permission_for_this_condition"):
  perform_action()
```
Here, it's more clear that the indented code block can only run if the user has satisfied the given conditions. In the first case, the block is entered even if the permission doesn't hold (until the imperative assertion is hit).

One alternative could be just to have a `has_permission` utility function, but then every use would need to throw the perms error itself instead of having the util method do it for them.

I disagree with this because the API is meant to check if that major version is being used, and the API should not be anything more flexible or complex than that.

Rather than wanting us to allow arbitrary interpreter constraints like `@skip_if_interpreter_is_any_python_version(">=3.4,<3.7")`, I think it's better to be more explicit with `@skip_if_interpreter_is_any_python_version("3.4", "3.5", "3.6")`.

We should start with the simplest API possible, and only make it more flexible if there's a compelling reason we need it. Right now, we're trying to only solve the problem of `skip if python37`, not a problem like `skip if python34 up to python37`.

Further, it would be confusing to change what we expect the parameter for these functions to be, because it would diverge from the file's `skip_unless_pythonx_present` functions expectation.

This flexibility is necessary since there's at least one instances where the `*error_list` argument isn't given a value so it's necessary to make this parameter optional and when the `*error_list` parameter is defaulted to `None`, validations will pass when they should fail.

On line 389:
`self._raise_error(SchemaError, message)`
No 3rd argument given.

I looked into including the `format_error()` function into `_raise_error()`. Unfortunately there are some instances where `self._error` is accessed directly without being formatted (via `self._error.format(data)`) or set to `None` and other instances where the `*error_list` argument is given a value where `self._error` is neither directly accessed nor formatted.

One line 380 to 381:
`self._raise_error(SchemaError, message,
                                  format_error(self._error))`
A `SchemaError` is raised that formats or sets `self._error` to `None`

On line 395:
`self._raise_error(SchemaError, message, self._error)`
Another `SchemaError` is raised, except `self._error` is accessed directly.

There are also other instances where various schema errors access `self._error` both directly and after formatting it.

On line 376-377:
`self._raise_error(SchemaError, [None] + x.autos,
                                  [self._error] + x.errors)`
`self._error` is neither formatted, set to none, nor just simply accessed directly.

So for the above reasons I didn't want `_raise_error()` to perform `format_error()` on the argument given for `*error_list` every time. And since `format_error()` is both applied and not applied for the same type of schema errors, I can't easily set `format_error()` to only be executed based on the exception that's raised.

The only solution I can think of at the moment is to have `self._raise_error()` take in a fourth argument that "flags" whether `format_error()` should be executed on the `*error_list` argument.

It's at this point that I figured, for now at least, it's simpler to leave `format_error()` external to `self._reaise_error()`

- If the requested metric is "report" and the model is a classifier, then just return the confusion matrix, report by class, and accuracy.
- If the requested metric is "report" and the model is not a classifier, then treat "report" as "auto

```python
>>> str(Path('this-.-looks--.-weird-'))
'"this-"."-looks--"."-weird-"'
```

> In general, are implementations of DataProvider allowed to have
> internal logic beyond, say, requesting a whole object from GCS and
> passing it along to the caller without processing? Is the answer is
> yes, then begin and end can be arbitrary valid integer values, because
> the DataProvider impl and take care of making multiple requests to the
> backend and slicing/dicing the results to fulfill the given begin and
> end values.

They’re not expected to, no. The point of the data provider abstraction
is to decouple the storage layer from the plugin space, turning an
*m*-times-*n* problem into an *m*-plus-*n* problem. We don’t want to
write and maintain extra code for the debugger plugin for each storage
layer (local TensorBoard’s from-disk reading, TensorBoard.dev, any
internal TensorBoard hostd instances…). This is why I’ve been calling
out all the cases where the plugin is implicitly depending on undefined
or special-cased behavior from this data provider, because when we put
in a real data provider it _won’t_ have any debugger-specific code.

> The `DebuggerV2Plugin` just needs to do the work of making multiple
> `read_blob()` calls + slicing/dicing the results according to begin
> and end.

Yes, and as @stephanwlee pointed out, you’ll want to be careful about
the total number of `read_blob` RPCs needed to service one request here.

So this is what tensorflow says - 
seeds can be set at graph-level and op-level.

To generate different sequences across sessions, set neither graph-level nor op-level seeds:
```
a = tf.random.uniform([1])
b = tf.random.normal([1])

print("Session 1")
with tf.compat.v1.Session() as sess1:
  print(sess1.run(a))  # generates 'A1'
  print(sess1.run(a))  # generates 'A2'
  print(sess1.run(b))  # generates 'B1'
  print(sess1.run(b))  # generates 'B2'

print("Session 2")
with tf.compat.v1.Session() as sess2:
  print(sess2.run(a))  # generates 'A3'
  print(sess2.run(a))  # generates 'A4'
  print(sess2.run(b))  # generates 'B3'
  print(sess2.run(b))  # generates 'B4'
```
To generate the same repeatable sequence for an op across sessions, set the seed for the op:
```
a = tf.random.uniform([1], seed=1)
b = tf.random.normal([1])

# Repeatedly running this block with the same graph will generate the same
# sequence of values for 'a', but different sequences of values for 'b'.
print("Session 1")
with tf.compat.v1.Session() as sess1:
  print(sess1.run(a))  # generates 'A1'
  print(sess1.run(a))  # generates 'A2'
  print(sess1.run(b))  # generates 'B1'
  print(sess1.run(b))  # generates 'B2'

print("Session 2")
with tf.compat.v1.Session() as sess2:
  print(sess2.run(a))  # generates 'A1'
  print(sess2.run(a))  # generates 'A2'
  print(sess2.run(b))  # generates 'B3'
  print(sess2.run(b))  # generates 'B4'
```

The supposed advantage of the way I've written it, and the reason that PyLint gives a warning if you don't, is that the string interpolation is lazy. I took a stab at this but it turns out not to be so simple. The reason is that we always have `**kwargs` in the signature, so even if someone writes a wrong method like `def on_batch_begin(net, X=None, y=None, **kwargs)`, it would not immediately lead to an error. The error occurs only when they do _something_ with X or y, because these will be None instead of the expected values. But this _something_ can be anything.

In my attempt, I just caught any kind of `Exception` and raised a `TypeError` from it that pointed at the possible error source. However, this broke other tests, which checked for specific errors (for instance, using scoring with a non existing function name). We don't want that to happen.

The problem is thus that we don't know what error will occur but we also don't want to catch all kinds of errors.

We could theoretically inspect the arguments inside the `notify`-wrapper. But there I would be afraid that the performance penalty could be too big. Furthermore, we want to allow the users to be very liberal in how they call their `on_*` methods (e.g. only passing the arguments that they need, which might not even include `batch`), so it's not possible to do a strict check.

This leaves me with no idea how to implement the suggestion. Do you have any ideas?

I worked up an extended set of tests, the first of which (`test_binding_strategy`) I intend to transcribe to doctest format and migrate into the ["Namespaces and bindings" document](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/docs/namespaces_and_bindings.rst) (I'll probably do the same with `test_multigraph_bindings` because it's more of an illustration than a test).

```py
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import pytest

from rdflib.term import URIRef
from rdflib import ConjunctiveGraph, Graph, Literal
from rdflib.plugins.stores.memory import Memory
from rdflib.namespace import Namespace, NAMESPACE_PREFIXES_CORE, NAMESPACE_PREFIXES_RDFLIB
from rdflib.namespace import OWL, RDFS
from test.data import tarek, michel, context0, context1, context2

foaf1_uri = URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/")
foaf2_uri = URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/")

FOAF1 = Namespace(foaf1_uri)
FOAF2 = Namespace(foaf2_uri)

def test_binding_strategy():

    # The confusion here is in the two arguments “override” and “replace” and
    # how they serve two different purposes --- changing a prefix already bound
    # to a namespace versus changing a namespace already bound to a prefix.

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")
    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) == 0

    # 1. Changing the namespace of an existing namespace=>prefix binding:

    # Say they release FOAF 2.0 and you want to change the binding of
    # `http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/` to `foaf`.

    # Up to now you had `http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/=>foaf` and `http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/=>foaf2`

    # We'll just set up those two bindings ...
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF1)
    g.bind("foaf2", FOAF2)
    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) == 2
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf2", foaf2_uri)
    ]

    # Now you want to "upgrade" to FOAF2=>foaf and try the obvious:
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2)

    # But that doesn't happen, instead a different prefix, `foaf1` is bound:
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf1", foaf2_uri)
    ]

    # The rationale behind this behaviour is that the prefix "foaf" was already
    # bound to the FOAF1 namespace, so a different prefix for the FOAF2 namespace 
    # was automatically created.

    # You can achieve the intended result by setting `replace` to `True`:
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2, replace=True)

    # Because the FOAF2 namespace was rebound to a different prefix, the old 
    # "foaf2" prefix has gone and because the "foaf" prefix was rebound to a
    # different namespace, the old FOAF1 namespace has gone, leaving just:

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri)
    ]

    # Now, if you wish, you can re-bind the FOAF1.0 namespace to a prefix
    # of your choice
    g.bind("oldfoaf", FOAF1)

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('oldfoaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # 2. Changing the prefix of an existing namespace=>prefix binding:

    # The namespace manager preserves the existing bindings from any
    # subsequent unwanted programmatic rebindings such as:
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF1)

    # Which, as things stand, results in:

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('foaf1', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # In which the attempted change from `oldfoaf` to (the already 
    # bound-to-a different-namespace `foaf`) was intercepted and
    # changed to a non-clashing prefix of `foaf1`

    # So, after undoing the above unwanted rebinding ..
    g.bind("oldfoaf", FOAF1, replace=True)

    # The bindings are again as desired 
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('oldfoaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # Next time through, set override to False
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF1, override=False)

    # And the bindings will remain as desired
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('oldfoaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # 3. Parsing data with prefix=>namespace bindings 
    # Let's see the situation regarding namespace bindings
    # in parsed data - it can be a bit confusing ...

    # Starting with a very likely example where `foaf` is a
    # prefix for `FOAF1`

    data = """\
    @prefix foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .

    <urn:example:a> foaf:name "Bob" ."""

    g.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    # The FOAF2 namespace is already bound to `foaf` so a
    # non-clashing prefix of `foaf1` is rebound to FOAF1 in
    # place of the existing `oldfoaf` prefix

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('foaf1', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # Yes, namespace bindings in parsed data replace existing
    # bindings (i.e. `oldfoaf` was replaced by `foaf1`), just
    # live with it ...

    # A different example of the same principle where `foaf2`
    # replaces the exsting `foaf`

    data = """\
    @prefix foaf2: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/> .

    <urn:example:b> foaf2:name "Alice" ."""

    g.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    # The already-bound namespace of `foaf=>FOAF2` is replaced
    # by the `foaf2=>FOAF2` binding specified in the N3 source

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf1', foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf2", foaf2_uri),
    ]

    # Where a prefix-namespace binding in the data does
    # not clash with the existing binding ...

    data = """\
    @prefix foaf1: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .

    <urn:example:a> foaf1:name "Bob" ."""

    g.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    # The prefix `foaf1`, already bound to FOAF1 is
    # used

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf1', foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf2", foaf2_uri),
    ]

    # Otherwise, the existing prefix binding is replaced

    data = """\
    @prefix foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .

    <urn:example:b> foaf:name "Alice" ."""

    g.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf2", foaf2_uri),
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri),
    ]

    # Prefixes are bound to namespaces which in turn have a URIRef
    # representation - so a different prefix=>namespace binding
    # means a different predicate and thus a different statement:

    expected = """
        @prefix foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .
        @prefix foaf2: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/> .

        <urn:example:a> foaf:name "Bob" .

        <urn:example:b> foaf:name "Alice" ;
            foaf2:name "Alice" .

        """

    s = g.serialize(format="n3")

    for l in expected.split():
        assert l in s


def test_prefix_alias_disallowed():

    # CANNOT BIND A PREFIX ALIASED TO AN EXISTING BOUND NAMESPACE

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")
    g.bind("owl", OWL)
    assert ('owl', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) in list(g.namespaces())

    g.bind("wol", OWL, override=False)
    assert ('owl', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) in list(g.namespaces())
    assert ('wol', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) not in list(g.namespaces())


def test_rebind_prefix_succeeds():

    # CAN REPLACE A PREFIX-NAMESPACE BINDING

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")
    g.bind("owl", OWL)
    assert ('owl', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) in list(g.namespaces())

    g.bind("wol", OWL)
    assert ('wol', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) in list(g.namespaces())
    assert ('owl', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')) not in list(g.namespaces())


def test_parse_rebinds_prefix():

    # PARSED PREFIX-NAMESPACE BINDINGS REPLACE EXISTING BINDINGS

    data = """@prefix friend-of-a-friend: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .

    <urn:example:a> friend-of-a-friend:name "Bob" .

    """

    # Use full set of namespace bindings, including foaf 
    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")
    g.bind('foaf', FOAF1)
    assert ('foaf', foaf1_uri) in list(g.namespaces())

    g.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    # foaf no longer in set of namespace bindings
    assert ('foaf', foaf1_uri) not in list(g.namespaces())
    assert ('friend-of-a-friend', foaf1_uri) in list(g.namespaces())


@pytest.mark.xfail(reason="Automatic handling of unknown predicates not automatically registered with namespace manager")
def test_automatic_handling_of_unknown_predicates():

    # AUTOMATIC HANDLING OF UNKNOWN PREDICATES

    """
    Automatic handling of unknown predicates
    -----------------------------------------

    As a programming convenience, a namespace binding is automatically
    created when :class:`rdflib.term.URIRef` predicates are added to the graph.
    """

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")

    g.add((tarek, URIRef('http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/name'), Literal("Tarek")))

    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) > 0


def test_automatic_handling_of_unknown_predicates_only_effected_after_serialization():

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")

    g.add((tarek, URIRef('http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/name'), Literal("Tarek")))

    assert "@prefix ns1: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> ." in g.serialize(format="n3")

    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) > 0
    assert ("ns1", URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/")) in list(g.namespaces())


def test_multigraph_bindings():
    data = """@prefix friend-of-a-friend: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> .

    <urn:example:a> friend-of-a-friend:name "Bob" .

    """

    store = Memory()

    g1 = Graph(store, identifier=context1, bind_namespaces="none")

    g1.bind('foaf', FOAF1)
    assert list(g1.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]
    assert list(store.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    g1.add((tarek, FOAF1.name, Literal("tarek")))

    assert list(store.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    g2 = Graph(store, identifier=context2, bind_namespaces="none")
    g2.parse(data=data, format="n3")

    # The parser-caused rebind is in the underlying store and all objects
    # that use the store see the changed binding:
    assert list(store.namespaces()) == [
        ('friend-of-a-friend', foaf1_uri)
    ]
    assert list(g1.namespaces()) == [
        ('friend-of-a-friend', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    # Including newly-created objects that use the store
    cg = ConjunctiveGraph(store=store)

    assert ('foaf', foaf1_uri) not in list(cg.namespaces())
    assert ('friend-of-a-friend', foaf1_uri) in list(cg.namespaces())

    assert len(list(g1.namespaces())) == 6
    assert len(list(g2.namespaces())) == 6
    assert len(list(cg.namespaces())) == 6
    assert len(list(store.namespaces())) == 6

    cg.store.add_graph(g1)
    cg.store.add_graph(g2)

    assert "@prefix friend-of-a-friend: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/>" in cg.serialize(format="n3")

    # In the notation3 format, the statement asserting tarek's name
    # now references the changed prefix:
    assert '<urn:example:tarek> friend-of-a-friend:name "tarek" .' in cg.serialize(format="n3")

    # Add foaf2 binding if not already bound
    cg.bind("foaf2", FOAF2, override=False)
    assert ('foaf2', foaf2_uri) in list(cg.namespaces())

    # Impose foaf binding ... if not already bound
    cg.bind("foaf", FOAF1, override=False)
    assert ('foaf', foaf1_uri) not in list(cg.namespaces())

        ("foaf1", foaf2_uri)
    ]


def test_new_namespace_override_false():
    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2)
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf2_uri)
    ]

    # Namespace not already bound so override is irrelevant in this case
    g.bind("owl", OWL, override=False)
    # Given prefix used
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ('owl', URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#')),
    ]


def test_change_namespace_and_prefix():

    # A more extensive illustration of attempted rebinds of
    # `foaf` being intercepted and changed to a non-clashing
    # prefix of `foafN` (where N is an incrementing integer) ...

    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF1)
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ('foaf', foaf1_uri)
    ]

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2, replace=True)
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri)
    ]

    g.bind("foaf1", FOAF1)

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ("foaf1", foaf1_uri)
    ]

    foaf3_uri = URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/3.0/")
    FOAF3 = Namespace("http://xmlns.com/foaf/3.0/")

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF3)

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ("foaf1", foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf2", foaf3_uri),
    ]

    foaf4_uri = URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/4.0/")
    FOAF4 = Namespace("http://xmlns.com/foaf/4.0/")

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF4)

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [
        ("foaf", foaf2_uri),
        ("foaf1", foaf1_uri),
        ("foaf2", foaf3_uri),
        ("foaf3", foaf4_uri),
    ]
```

@samskalicky The base is equal to the size of the diagonal. The output is reshaped to `oshape + (base,)`, and the input is reshaped to `ishape`. `idx` is therefore the part corresponding to `oshape` and `ishape`. And it corresponds to the mth element in the diagonal, where `m = i % base = I - idx * base`. We need to multiply it by `stride`, which is basically the sum of the strides of `axis1` and `axis2` in order compute the index of the element we want. However, because the diagonal we want is not necessarily the main diagonal (only when `k = 0`), we need to add to the result an offset.

> as the entire logic of `path_comparator` and `path_accessor` is honestly too complex for me to handle ATM.

The complexity stems from the fact that we cannot use lambdas or local function together with the datapipes, since they cannot be serialized safely. Quick explanation:

- `path_accessor`: When using datapipes you often have path-handle-tuples for files. `path_accessor` takes such a tuple, turns the the first item into a `pathlib.Path` and accesses it according to the input. So `path_accessor("parent.name")` is equivalent to

    ```python
    def extract_folder_name(data):
        path = pathlib.Path(data[0])
        return path.parent.name
    ```

    This is useful when you want to merge two datapipes and need a function that generates merge keys.
- `path_comparator`: This is one layer on top of `path_accessor` by providing an equality comparison for the extract path information. For example `Filter(scenes_dp, path_comparator("name", f"CLEVR_{config.split}_scenes.json"))` will select all files where the file name matches the second attribute. (This is what I used to refactor your suggestion about the regex).

But the `+1` here is intentional to ensure we reject a `aNameString` which has a length longer than `kMaxSize`.

- Basically `NetworkName::Data` has no restriction on what its `mLength` can be set to. 
- When setting from `aNameString` we only care about `kMaxSize + 1` chars in the string.
- When we use `SetNetworkName(data)` (which in turn calls `NetworkName::Set(data)` method), the `Set()` method will check for new length to be fit in `kMaxSize` (otherwise retursn `OT_ERROR_INVALID_ARGS`)
   -  `NetworkName::Set()` will check for any NULL chars within `data` and calculates the `newLen` for the name.

Classic REST will assign a unique ID on resource creation and then use that ID for subsequent requests. 

Accepting the entire spec for a delete() or similar call may seem harmless. But I have always believed that a good API has exactly one way to do a specific thing. Allowing to do it in multiple ways creates ambiguity, it can cause confusion, and it multiplies the amount of code that needs to be maintained (and kept backwards-compatible) in the future. 

One drawback of accepting a spec for get or delete etc. is that the user may think the entire spec must match, when in reality everything but ID is ignored. For example, a user may expect that the spec returned is equal to the spec passed in - that would not be the case. Or, on delete, the user may expect that it only deletes resources that match the whole spec, including, say, the name. But it will delete a pipeline with a matching ID, regardless of name. 

This kind of ambiguities can easily be avoided.

I'm not saying we shouldn't be using child classes, I'm saying we don't need the special `AbstractDataclass`, because using simple `abc.ABC` works just fine with dataclasses. This comment was purely about removing this class, I'm not suggesting to remove the base classes we're using, I'm just suggesting to use simple `abc.ABC` with them, instead of this custom class.

All this custom class does is prevent direct initialization, no matter if the dataclass has some abstract methods or not. This same behavior is normal for `abc.ABC`, no matter if the class is a dataclass or not. It does make sense, because only classes with some methods without implementation shouldn't be possible to get initialized, classes without these methods, even if they're just base classes doesn't need to have initialization prevented completely.

I just suggested adding these checks into java response classes because they would mean an abstract `build` method would be present in the base classes, hence even with just simple `abc.ABC`, they wouldn't be initializable. That class can also just be a wrapper around `__init__` and just return `cls(*a, **kw)`. or maybe it doesn't need to be there at all and initialization can be allowed.

If you want to consider removing these type checks completely, make an issue about that, or do it in another PR, this one is already very big and doesn't need any further changes like this, that's a different discussion completely.

You are right. Stop() needs to synchronously call the callback. So I made the "task posting" between "detach gracefully" and Stop(), instead of between Stop() and the callback.

I.e. from `DetachGracefully -> Stop -> Post -> callback` to `DetachGracefully -> Post -> Stop -> callback`.

When the callback is called, the state must be disabled.

```python
global_var = False


def global_keyword_test():
    global global_var
    if any(global_var := f[0] for f in [[True]]):
        print(global_var)


def global_no_keyword_test():
    if any(global_var := f[0] for f in [[True]]):
        print(global_var)


def nonlocal_keyword_test():
    local_var = False

    def helper():
        nonlocal local_var
        if any(local_var := f[0] for f in [[True]]):
            print(local_var)
    helper()
    print(local_var)


def nonlocal_no_keyword_test():
    local_var_2 = False

    def helper():
        if any(local_var_2 := f[0] for f in [[True]]):
            print(local_var_2)
    helper()
    print(local_var_2)


def original_issue():
    def helper():
        if any(local_var_v2 := f[0] for f in [[True]]):
            print(local_var_v2)
    helper()


print("global_keyword_test")
global_keyword_test()
print(global_var)
print("")
global_var = False

print("global_no_keyword_test")
global_no_keyword_test()
print(global_var)
print("")
global_var = False

print("nonlocal_keyword_test")
nonlocal_keyword_test()
print("")

print("nonlocal_no_keyword_test")
nonlocal_no_keyword_test()
print("")
```

> The change from PythonBinaryFields / PythonBinaryImplementation just looks like a non-obvious win

My bad with a confusing PR comment at the start of this thread. There are two things going on with this PR in regards to `PythonBinaryFields`:

1. Rename `PythonBinaryFields` to `PythonBinaryImplementation`. This is solely a name change to reflect what ended up being far more natural in real-world usage, e.g. what sounded better in the error message in the PR description.
   * This is what the original comment in this thread was referring to.
2. Have `PythonBinaryImplementation` subclass `BinaryImplementation`, whereas previously it was a stand-alone dataclass.
   * This removes some boilerplate. We no longer need to define `is_valid_target()` and `valid_target_types()` because the superclass gives us those.
   * This all still works with MyPy. We could probably remove even more boilerplate by putting more logic into the superclass, e.g. `create()`, but MyPy wouldn't be able to know what each field is, anymore.

I think #1 is stable. I don't think we'll want to change the name again. (But, we can, need be).

#2 is very fluid. I expect for there to be much churn the next week as we encounter new real-world use cases.

> I don't really like that inner layers depend on upper layers.

So I made two versions of how we can eliminate this dependency.

1. Avoid importing objects from `modin.pandas...` to the algebra level, but still allow passing such objects to the `Operator.apply()`. This way we're getting rid of the 'import dependency' on the higher level, meaning that we can easily detach the algebra layer if needed without worrying that it would require to bear stuff from the higher levels for the algebra to work correctly.

   I've made the changes to align with this approach and pushed it to the branch from this PR.

2. Also avoid passing dataframe objects to the `Operator.apply()` and rework this method to accept query compilers only. Then add a helper function somewhere to the dataframe level that would take series/dataframes, extract their QCs, and pass to them the `Operator.apply()`.

    I've implemented this approach in [a separate branch](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/commit/1a8f23e37a2441d1efc470ed7bde2f3526d805e1). There, users have a function at [`modin.pandas.utils.apply_operator`](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/1a8f23e37a2441d1efc470ed7bde2f3526d805e1/modin/pandas/utils.py#L375) with the following signature:

    ```python
   def apply_operator(operator_cls, *args, **kwargs):
        pass
    ...
    # use case example
    from modin.pandas.utils import apply_operator
    from modin.core.dataframe.algebra import Reduce

    series_obj = apply_operator(Reduce, df, reduce_func, axis=1)
   ```

    I don't really like this approach as `apply_operator()` doesn't provide meaningful signature and requires referring to the `Operator.apply()` for the list of allowed parameters.

The constructor needs to return immediately, so the go routine is launched to await either the closure of the `done` channel (which signifies that the logging has completed and `livelog` is about to be terminated) or for the `livelog` process to complete. If the `livelog` process completes before the `done` channel is closed, it means the livelog exited before we intentionally killed it. The output and the error returned by the `livelog` command are written to the `putResult` channel (line 83), so this new go routine's purpose is to capture the output and error of `livelog` if it completes before the `done` channel is closed, and report them to the standard error of the worker process.

This is how I discovered that the reason `livelog` was terminating early was due to the hardcoded port you added, which is fixed above in `workers/generic-worker/livelog.go`.

> now that we have `GapChange.GapFilled`, wouldn't it be better to simply make that extra check in Py-EVM when we fill a gap. I mean, load the existing known child (the checkpoint header) and if the ends don't match up, raise an exception.

Yup, that seems reasonable to me. So then we have to handle this scenario, with a series of inserted headers between A0 and A4, but the B branch is actually uncled. The top row is the presumed canonical one.

```
A0 -> __ -> __ -> __ -> A4

A0 -> B1 -> __ -> __ -> A4

A0 -> B1 -> B2 -> __ -> A4

A0 -> B1 -> B2 -> __ -> A4  # Tries to insert B3, but it's not a parent of A4, and raises an exception

# client somehow finds the A chain and starts inserting it
# it is deemed non-canonical at first (current PR functionality)
A0 -> B1 -> B2 -> __ -> A4
   \_ A1

A0 -> B1 -> B2 -> __ -> A4
   \_ A1 -> A2

# When inserting A3, pyevm notices that it fills a gap, and links with the child
# It notices that A3's parent is present but non-canonical, and makes 
# B* non-canonical and A[1-3] canonical
A0 -> A1 -> A2 -> A3 -> A4
   \_ B1 -> B2
```

As to leave no stone unturned, I've looked closer and found that `ddl.py` has its own module under `sqlalchemy/sql/ddl.py`, whereas query clauses seem to be mostly in `sqlalchemy/sql/selectable.py`.

Once we go into the dialect tho, `sqlalchemy/dialects/postgresql`, its the `base.py` where most of the dropping and creating happens (i.e. contains the DDL modifications for PostgreSQL).  Under  `sqlalchemy/dialects/postgresql`, there seem to be special modules for some of PostgreSQL's special types (json, array, etc.).

So imagine a user has something like this:
```
# app.py

from flask import Flask
import sentry_sdk

sentry_sdk.init(...)  # potel enabled
app = Flask()
```
The OTel autoinstrumentation runs during `sentry_sdk.init()`, patching the `Flask` class with its own `_InstrumentedFlask`, but since the user imported `Flask` before, in `app.py` it is still the old unpatched `Flask`, and so is `app`.

The loop goes through all modules loaded so far via `sys.modules` and does the following for each of the original classes:
* Tries reimporting them to see if what we import is instrumented or not. This is used to check if the autoinstrumentation was actually successful in the first place, because if not, we don't want to patch any leftover unpatched classes. (This check needs to be moved out of this loop since this is something that needs to only be checked once for a class, I will do that.)
* After having verified that the patching was successful, it then goes through the `vars` of each `sys.module` and checks whether the original type is in scope, and if so, replaces it with the patched type.

So in the above example, it would find the `app.py` module in `sys.modules`, it would check it for occurrences of the original `Flask` type, and replace them with `_InstrumentedFlask`.

```python
COMPONENTS_DATA = {
    'reana-workflow-controller': (
       'http://{address}:{port}'.format(
           address=os.getenv('WORKFLOW_CONTROLLER_SERVICE_HOST', '0.0.0.0'),
           port=os.getenv('WORKFLOW_CONTROLLER_SERVICE_PORT_HTTP', '5000')),
       'reana_workflow_controller.json'),
```

We could have a mapping here in commons which would look like:
```python
OPENAPI_COMPONENTS = {
    'reana-workflow-controller': (
        'http://{address}:{port}'.format(
            address=os.getenv('WORKFLOW_CONTROLLER_SERVICE_HOST', '0.0.0.0'),

}
```

I don't think we can support a TRUNCATE call here right now. There would be an issue if a user wrote an asset, materialized it, then changed the code to add a new column. When we re-materialize the asset, if we just truncate the table, the underlying snowflake table will still have the columns of the original materialization. When we try to load the new dataframe with the new column, we will get an error because the new dataframe column doesn't have a corresponding column in the snowflake table. 

Unfortunately, the library we are using to load dataframes (pandas.to_sql with the snowflake pandas writer) doesn't handle adding new columns. We have an issue open [here](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/issues/13098) to support adding new columns for partitions (where we have the same issue). Once we implement this, then we could switch to using TRUNCATE here as well

The actual interface for getting at exit status and signal numbers is documented [here](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/waitpid.2.html).

```
WIFEXITED(wstatus)
              returns true if the child terminated normally, that is, by
              calling exit(3) or _exit(2), or by returning from main().

       WEXITSTATUS(wstatus)
              returns the exit status of the child.  This consists of the
              least significant 8 bits of the status argument that the child
              specified in a call to exit(3) or _exit(2) or as the argument
              for a return statement in main().  This macro should be
              employed only if WIFEXITED returned true.

       WIFSIGNALED(wstatus)
              returns true if the child process was terminated by a signal.

       WTERMSIG(wstatus)
              returns the number of the signal that caused the child process
              to terminate.  This macro should be employed only if WIFSIG‐
              NALED returned true.
```

There's an exit status *only* if the child process terminated normally, and a signal number *only* if the child process was terminated by a signal. These cases are mutually exclusive: if the child calls exit() from a signal handler, you'll see a normal termination and not a signal termination. That's how Bash gets away with packing these both into one integer.

I'm not sure if Slurm will report something else for the case where e.g. it sent the child a signal, but the child managed to exit normally before receiving it.

I think if the exit code is nonzero we should report only that, and if the exit code is zero and the signal is nonzero we should report `128 + signal` as Bash does.

I don't care about this too much, but I'd prefer if we used `pytest` here instead of the unittest-specific stuff. I also strongly prefer a context manager form. The `pytest` version of this is:

```python
with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="unsupported property"):
    rrulestr(rule, dtstart=datetime(1997, 9, 2, 9, 0))
```

That said, there are two other issues here:

1. We care less about the "unsupported property" message than that we've picked out the _correct_ unsupported property.
2. The `dtstart=datetime(1997, 9, 2, 9, 0)` portion of this is unnecessary, and including it might confuse the reader into thinking that it's an important part of the test.

So, I suggest refactoring this test like so:

```python
def testRaisesCorrectErrorForUnsupportedProperty(self):
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match=re.escape("X-ABC-MMSUBJ")):
        rrulestr("X-ABC-MMSUBJ:19970902T090000")
```

> I wanted to include globals so that preamble authors would have access to the functions in the bootstrap/__init__.py module 

Shiv is included in the archive, so if someone wants access to the functions, it would make more sense IMO to just import them, e.g. `from shiv.bootstrap import cache_path`.

> But perhaps for starters we should be more conservative and just plug in some specific stuff like {"site_packages": site_packages, "environment": env}. What do you think? I'm totally open to ideas here.

You are going to be creating an API "contract" based on what variables/data are made available to the preamble script.  Easy to add things, but taking things away means a potential breaking change.  Therefore, IMO, I'd start lean and add things in the future if really needed.  Additionally, I wouldn't want to tie the name of the variables that are passed to the preamble script directly to the variable names being used in the bootstrap module and functions.   Therefore, I'd probably start with something simple like:

```python
init_globals={
    'shiv_environment': env, 
    'shiv_site_packages_path': site_packages, 
    'shiv_cache_path': site_packages.parent
})
```
I prefixed with "shiv_" since they are going to end up in the calling script's global space, less likely to have collisions that way.  I might consider UPPERCASE for the variables too.  That is usually reserved for constants, which isn't exactly true here, but I like the fact that the uppercase gives some indication that they are "special" since to the calling script, they are a bit magical...i.e. they are kind of coming out of thin air.

I would not add any of the global variables in.  IMO it's not needed and may add to confusion later.

> So, whichever solution you land on, we hope it supports modifying the environment so that we can dynamically set the entry point at runtime crossed_fingers

FWIW, this wouldn't be my preferred approach.  I want Shiv to do the work of setting up the shiv environment and extracting the packages before the preamble is called.  

For @luislew's use case, having a more flexible entry point that can "do the right thing" based on whether the script has arguments or not would be the better solution, instead of being able to modify which entry point was called with the preamble script.  

It looks like `run_path()` supports returning the global's of the called script and you could therefore permit the preamble to modify the environment here in the bootstrap function by doing something with it's return value.  I guess it just depends on whether or not you want to support that kind of use case.

I've given this some thought, and I wonder if it makes sense to expose the ```EventContinuationToken``` to the user since we don't know how to deterministically generate such a token from an ```event_id```. That is, we could envision a scenario where a user requests events older than an ```event_id``` which isn't the oldest (e.g., requesting events older than event 47 when only the first 50 have been received), but it would not be possible to do the same using the continuation token (in the same example, the token received could only be used to request events 51-100). 

I believe it would be more straightforward to keep the token internal to ```Conversation``` objects, using them only to pull events from the server as needed.

So I disagree with that.  Although `LogWriter` is defined in your internal subpackage, we are calling it publicly: `tracer.writer.writer()` from `ddtrace.tracer`.  

`ddtrace.__init__` instantiates a public global tracer:
```
# a global tracer instance with integration settings
tracer = Tracer()
```

`writer` is a public member on `Tracer` which is an a instantiation of `LogWriter`, and `LogWriter.write()` is a public method.  

So I get that you are putting your class definition in a "private" subpackage, however since you are making a global instance of that public via a chain of public members ultimately made public at the top level, it is not fair game as you say.  I don't think it's correct procedure for developers to have to inspect every public API member to see where the class definition is before using it. I have already applied this concept:
* `CheckpointManager` is solely responsible for managing checkpoints.
* `CheckpointManagerWithSince` is a container for the `since` value and checkpoint manager specific to the current table export.
* `CheckpointProvider` is responsible for creating new the creating a new `CheckpointManagerWithSince` when requested with the `since` value and the checkpoint manager.

The `since` value and the checkpoints are inextricably tied together because unless specified at the command line the `since` value comes from the checkpoints which is why it makes sense to be part of the checkpoint manager: https://github.com/dimagi/commcare-export/pull/121/files#diff-a29d76e0f5317a0f68900178498d8141R314

Having said all that I'm open to suggestions for improvement. I did got through several iterations with this and originally didn't have the `CheckpointManagerWithSince`. I introduced that class so that the checkpoint manager with tables set would only live inside that class which in turn would only live inside the context of the of `Bind`.

No what I mean is that it should never be checked in (similar to how `local` will be checked in), but it is used locally to download the ZIP without knowing the hash a priori. And as a bonus Bazel tells you what the hash is when you do that, so it's quite easy to just copy-paste from the terminal that's already in front of you. Ok so let's say that you have a dict like `dict1 = {'first_key':'first_value', 'second_key':'second_value'}`. Now if you do something like `third_value = dict1['third_key']`, Python will raise a  `KeyError` exception since in `dict1` the key `third_key` is not defined. To handle this exception you have to do something like

```python
try:
    third_value = dict1['third_key']
except KeyError:
    print("dict1 has not third_key as key")
```

Now if you don't specify `except KeyError`, `except:` will handle every possible exception, like `KeyboardInterrupt` (when you hit `CTRL + C`)

Basically you should check that library and see which exceptions could be raised, and handle that specific one

This block specifically addresses function calls of locally defined functions. We're interested in them because they could affect the redundancy of an assignment statement (think `nonlocal` variables). The logic is that if a call to a locally defined function call is made and the local variables of that function are not a subset of `gen`, then we `kill` all the `gen` variables. This is because there is a possibility of usage of one of the `gen` variables within that function definition.

I strongly dislike forcing use of a hidden test API when NumPy's is perfectly suitable.  Where certain features may not exist at base in NumPy, we should publicly expose these (even if they are deeply nested, like `skimage.util.testing` or similar.  The potential portability advantages are not significant enough to justify this.  This is the same reason we generally don't reimplement `scipy.ndimage`.

Imagine a new user making a first contribution.  They generate a perfectly compliant test suite using guides they find online, via NumPy's suite.  We tell them - despite technical correctness - that we won't accept it?  Yikes.  All this really does is provide additional barriers to contribution.

Barriers to contribution are high enough already, IMHO, and we should be looking to reduce them.

I've briefly considered allowing `env["::include_path"]` to return collector values (same with queries), however, I felt it clashed with the dict semantics of the options (raise exception on `env[option]`, return default on `env.get(option, default)`).

Calls to `env.collector(key)` and `env.query(key)` now raise exceptions when the key doesn't exists, since these are likely to not have a default that can fit into one-line argument.
Instead you should to use `env.has_collector(key)` and `env.has_query(key)` and do the default programmatically.

Also note that `env.collector_values(key, default=None)` raises an exception if the key is not found and default is `None`. I found this behavior very useful, since it prevents silent failures.
The return value is listified, so passing `default=None` would output `[None]`, so I chose to use this to check for the key. You can pass `default=[]` or `default=[None]` if you do not want that exception.

```python
>>> dt.shape
(1, 2, 3)

(dtype('int64'), (1, 2, 3))
```

I would probably also add a comment about how, *like* classes, only one object with a given `__module__` and `__name__` can be pickled at a time. 

```pycon
>>> class Foo:
...     pass
>>> f1 = Foo
>>> class Foo:
...     pass
>>> pickle.dumps(f1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
PicklingError: Can't pickle <class '__main__.Foo'>: it's not the same object as __main__.Foo
```

 If you do this dynamically without watching the assigned names, none can be pickled.
```pycon
>>> f1 = type('Foo', (), {})
>>> f2 = type('Foo', (), {})
>>> pickle.dumps(f1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
PicklingError: Can't pickle <class '__main__.Foo'>: it's not the same object as __main__.Foo
>>> del Foo
>>> pickle.dumps(f2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
PicklingError: Can't pickle <class '__main__.Foo'>: attribute lookup Foo on __main__ failed
```

All of the above holds for interfaces.

Classes, however, are inconsistent, or at least inconvenient: They compare and hash by identity (and can't be sorted) but pickle by global identifier, meaning that the value of `==` can change. 
```pycon
>>> class Foo:
...     pass
>>> f1 = Foo
>>> pickled_f1 = pickle.dumps(Foo)
>>> class Foo:
...     pass
>>> f1 == Foo
False
>>> unpickled_f1 = pickle.loads(pickled_f1)
>>> unpickled_f1
<class '__main__.Foo'>
>>> unpickled_f1 == Foo # Surprise!
True
>>> unpickled_f1 == f1
False
```

This is not the case for interfaces:
```pycon
>>> class IFoo(Interface):
...     pass
>>> f1 = IFoo
>>> pickled_f1 = pickle.dumps(f1)
>>> class IFoo(Interface):
...     pass
>>> IFoo == f1
True
>>> unpickled_f1 = pickle.loads(pickled_f1)
>>> unpickled_f1
<InterfaceClass __main__.IFoo>
>>> unpickled_f1 == IFoo == f1
True
```
Yes. It is much clearer

If >1 target "owns" the same module, Pants dep inference will no-op:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/824a15daa9f633554b5544ae2c59405601e20e1c/src/python/pants/backend/python/dependency_inference/module_mapper.py#L196-L198

This is really important that we do this, as Pants cannot disambiguate what the user intended. For example, sometimes a user will have a version of Numpy with CPU, and another with GPU. It's an error to load both at the same time. We can't disambiguate which the user wants, so we don't try to be too clever, and the user must explicitly add the dependency.

It does not really depend on any specific environment. The problem is that `vsc-install` is breaking any scripts that can only be executed in Python 3. Scripts with a shebang such as `#!/usr/bin/env python3` are replaced with `#!/usr/bin/python-stripped-env` which is hardcoded to `/usr/bin/python`. Therefore, if the Python 3 interpreter is called `python3`, as it usually is, things break.

I propose to solve it by allowing to pass an argument to `python-stripped-env` defining the target interpreter. In a similar way to how `/usr/bin/env` works. This PR does not change the default behaviour of `python-stripped-env`.

If you have the schema:
```yaml
not:
    required: [a, b]
```
then `{a: stuff}` and `{b: stuff}` will both validate, but `{'a: stuff, b:stuff}` will not. That is because both the first two do *not* validate against `required: [a, b]` (since in either case they are missing one of the required properties).

The way that `{not: {}}` works is that `{}` will match against any value (including `null`). So, `not: {}` will not match against any value. Therefore, if an optional property has to match `not: {}`, it can't be specified (since the value specified would not match the schema).

When there are multiple traversals the first element of the list will be a join from the outer vertex to some other vertex, and the last element of the list will be a join from some other vertex to the output vertex, with a number of other joins in between traversing from outer to output.

```python
assert key in dict # Checking that key in dictionary
value = dict[key]  # Getting value from a dict, which also implies
		   # checking for the existence of the key.
```
So we checked that the key in a dict for two times, why is that inconsistency? Some bad things could happen with a dict between these two lines:
- Somebody inserts some code that modifies dict
- Even if we're going to watch closely at the future reviews that these two lines always come after each other that doesn't save us from the problem that if one day this code will be used in parallel, the thread could be paused after the successful assert check and then the other thread will modify a dict, so we'll get a `KeyError` when returning to the first thread (the operation is not atomic)

This approach solves all of the problems:
```python
try:
    value = dict[key]
except KeyError:
    raise KeyError(error_explanation)
```

Although the timings are hardly significant here, it seems that the dictionary approach is slightly faster:

```py
import torch
import itertools
from time import perf_counter_ns


@torch.jit.script
def max_value_dict(dtype: torch.dtype) -> int:
    return {
        torch.uint8: int(2 ** 8) - 1,
        torch.int8: int(2 ** 7) - 1,
        torch.int16: int(2 ** 15) - 1,
        torch.int32: int(2 ** 31) - 1,
        torch.int64: int(2 ** 63) - 1,
    }.get(dtype, 1)


@torch.jit.script
def max_value_if(dtype: torch.dtype) -> int:
    if dtype == torch.uint8:
        return int(2 ** 8) - 1
    elif dtype == torch.int8:
        return int(2 ** 7) - 1
    elif dtype == torch.int16:
        return int(2 ** 15) - 1
    elif dtype == torch.int32:
        return int(2 ** 31) - 1
    elif dtype == torch.int64:
        return int(2 ** 63) - 1
    else:
        return 1


num = 1_000_000

dict_timings = []
if_timings = []

for dtype in itertools.islice(
    itertools.cycle(torch.testing._legacy.get_all_dtypes(include_bool=False, include_complex=False)), num
):
    tic = perf_counter_ns()
    max_value_dict(dtype)
    tac = perf_counter_ns()
    dict_timings.append(tac - tic)

    tic = perf_counter_ns()
    max_value_if(dtype)
    tac = perf_counter_ns()
    if_timings.append(tac - tic)

print(f"{torch.tensor(dict_timings, dtype=torch.float64).mean() * 1e-3:.3f} µs per call using dicts")
print(f"{torch.tensor(if_timings, dtype=torch.float64).mean() * 1e-3:.3f} µs per call using ifs")
```

```
0.990 µs per call using dicts
1.100 µs per call using ifs
```

Right now, most renderers are sending the progress since session start instead, so when `addProgress` is used for updates, it leads to updates like this:

progress += 2%
progress += 4%
progress += 6%
progress += 8%
progress += 10%

So after five progress updates, instead of progress being the expected 10%, it is actually 30%.

Yep, or better description is [in the spec](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#AuthRequest). Its used to prevent replay attacks, the client generates a random nonce and stores it in a session, and then compares the nonce in the response with the nonce in the session. If it is missing either in the session or the response, or does not match, the response is invalid.

Its a required parameter for the implicit flow, a sometimes required parameter for the hybrid flow and an optional parameter for authorization code flow.

Handling it, however, is not optional - if a nonce is presented in the authorization endpoint, it must be in the ID token or a client should reject the response as invalid.

The deletion logic isn't for when the user wants to install ray, it's for when they put something like `ray_xgboost` in their pip list, which has `ray>=1.6` in its dependencies.  When we install `ray_xgboost`, it'll install the latest Ray into the target directory, and that will be prioritized over whatever Ray was already installed on the cluster.  So if the user is running anything other than the latest Ray, it'll fail with a version mismatch error.  

The environment variable is an escape hatch from the deletion logic, for when the user does for some reason want to include `ray` in their pip list.

After reading about the loggers I could understand the following (Please correct me if I am wrong):
1. When loggers are instantiated using `logging.getLogger()` without specifying a name (as `logging.getLogger(name)`)  it applies the configuration to the __root logger__ (in main module) and also to the parser.py module logger due to which `logger.setLevel` (for root logger) here is configured to DEBUG and/or WARNING. So it worked. 
2. If I replace `getLogger()` in the main module by `getLogger(__name__)`, the logger in the parsers.py module will become a child of the root logger and it will thus inherit the root logger configuration and as the root logger has `setLevel` configured to `logging.INFO` it will log INFO messages.

Yes, I think this is described in pydantic_core somewhere (I wanted documentation because it confused the crap out of me when I first saw it), but I'll add a note here about what it means. The idea for discriminators in general:
* `str`: name of property to grab to obtain discriminator value
* `int`: index to grab (for arrays) to obtain discriminator value
* `list[str | int]`: obtain the discriminator value by iteratively/recursively applying the items of the list to the value obtained following the instructions in the first two bullets above
* `list[list[str | int]]`: follow the procedure described in the previous bullet for each item in the outer list until you get a valid discriminator value

So, if you need to check multiple fields to grab the discriminator value (as is the case if populate_by_name is True and there is an alias), the way to do this is to use the list-of-lists approach (representing alternative choices) where the inner lists represent accessing the specific property that you want to grab the discriminator from.

Fun fact: `[attribute!=value]` is a cssselect-specific extension which is not in any CSS standard. This also means its behavior is not described anywhere. So let's look how it works:

```
In [3]: GenericTranslator().css_to_xpath('[foo!=bar]')
Out[3]: "descendant-or-self::*[not(@foo) or @foo != 'bar']"

In [5]: GenericTranslator().css_to_xpath('[foo!=""]')
Out[5]: "descendant-or-self::*[@foo != '']"
```

So valid matches for non-empty values are an absent attribute and an attribute that isn't equal to the value. The only valid match for an empty value is a present non-empty attribute. We should keep this behavior.

This also applies to `xpath_attrib_equals` above, where value can't be None but can be an empty string.

The use case you're describing is not very likely. Interactive mode will mostly be used through the CLIs, not standalone with the SDK. This is why we are not adding interactive mode to the other auth libraries. That being said, if they want to use it standalone, they can, but they need to specify an interactive mode configuration - consistent behavior with gcloud. 

I don't see any benefit of adding a default value for this optional field. I think adding it results in a worse experience as now the user's timeout is not transparent to them (e.g. they can set interactive to true on the creds but think the executable timeout is the non interactive timeout in the config since we're now mixing both). It complicates an already complicated flow, adds more documentation burden, and may cause issues in the future with the gcloud integration.

I don't remember your exact gcloud changes for this since they were deleted, but if this isn't breaking that doesn't really change my opinion on this. Relying on `interactive_timeout_millis` to be set in the config and then setting a default value here is error prone.

Here is my hypothesis:
The TM1 server does some kind of "scanning" when a process is created. That operation is expensive in terms of performance and the cost scales linear with the number of lines in the process.
https://github.com/cubewise-code/tm1py/issues/774#issuecomment-1258088290

In the old approach, we would create a process with 1 million lines. Now we always create a process with 15 lines or so. Even if we write 1 million elements.

Do you have any idea to implement `schemaVersion` field?

Ideally, we can introduce `schemaVersion` field at the top level as CWL spec does.
For the implicit context, it is easily implemented it by adding a schema for it.

The problem is when introducing it to the explicit context.
The explicit context accepts the `$base`, `$namespaces`, `$schemas`, and `$graph` fields and others are ignored.

In the current spec of SALAD, in my understanding, there are no way to introduce extra fields to the explicit context by the (meta) schema.
On the other hand, once we hard-code to accept the `schemaVersion` field in the explicit context, the packed CWL documents accidentally accept the `schemaVersion` field.

---
One workaround is just to introduce a new field `schemaVersion` to `LoadingOptions` and to add a new command line option such as `--schema-version` to `schema-salad-tool`.
At least we can implement it with a small effort and can provide a new feature to users soon.

I think it won't work when we have all three booleans as `false` (i.e., `forwardHost == false`, `receive == false`, and `forwardThread == false`). In this case, `shouldFreeMessage` will be set to `false` but we should still free the message.

---

The way we use and set `shouldFreeMessage` is kinda of tricky. 
- We set `shouldFreeMessage = true` at the top (basically we always want to free the message).
- Only when we call `PassToHost()` and `HandlePayload` and use `kTakeCustody` we pass ownership of `Message` (and we expect the method we called to free it), we need to set the `shouldFreeMessage` to `false` to avoid double free.

I plan/hope to later change and simplify this. The idea is to use `Message *` and when we pass ownership we set the pointer to `nullptr` - at the end we free if it is not `nullptr`.

```python
import timeit
```

```
├── example-pex
│   ├── DEBIAN

│   │   └── company
│   │       └── example

│           └── do-work -> /opt/company/example/example-linux-cp3.6.pex
```

user can specify the name of a symlink to be created which will point to the PEX file. In light of the redesign plan, I removed my request for changes, but please note that use of the `buttonLinkStyle` in general has a11y issues.  It looks like a link, but maps to a button role in the accessibility tree, leading to issues such as:

- Someone using voice software like Dragon Naturally Speaking might give the commend to "click the manage aliases link", which doesn't work because there is no such link.
- Documentation or a user forum might help a screen reader user by saying click the link to manage aliases.  They then use screen reader shortcuts to navigate through links, and get confused when they don't find it.

Aside from that, there's the potential implication to users with cognitive disabilities which are always more difficult to define concretely.

In my opinion we definitely should have a retry mechanism in place. This would avoid slow payments due to channel failure. Imagine a channel goes down because of a connection issue. Then we should wait for the next healthcheck (which might occur upon the next payment) resulting in a slow payment. 

We should rather retry a fixed amount of times (lets say 3 times) if a channel does not get created, we can assume that the partner is offline and stop retrying, waiting for the next health check. 

This optimistic approach of retrying prevents issues for example when a peer roams to a different network or the network connection breaks (which happens once in a while)

> _HasDefaultComparison is defined as a new class above

Right. Specifically, it's defined as a new abstract base class, i.e., something that extends `abc.ABCMeta`. (We could do this with a regular `type` subclass, but extending `ABCMeta` gets us caching of the results of `isinstance` automatically.)

> where is `item` registered for it, so this check can become true?

That's the beauty part (as the saying goes). By making `_HasDefaultComparison` implement `__subclasshook__`, we [algorithmically define](https://docs.python.org/3/library/abc.html#abc.ABCMeta.__subclasshook__) what `isinstance(item, _HasDefaultComparison)` means. No need to register anything! The algorithm is the same as what was here before, just moved into `__subclasshook__`. (The expression `isinstance(item, Cls)` basically boils down to `Cls.__subclasscheck__(type(item))`; `ABCMeta` defines `__subclasscheck__` to do some caching, and if the answer is not in the cache, to call `Cls.__subclasshook__()`.)

```
from cvxpy.tests.solver_test_helpers import StandardTestLPs
from contextlib import contextmanager
import tempfile
import sys
import os


def fileno(file_or_fd):
    fd = getattr(file_or_fd, 'fileno', lambda: file_or_fd)()
    if not isinstance(fd, int):
        raise ValueError("Expected a file (`.fileno()`) or a file descriptor")
    return fd


@contextmanager
def stdout_redirected(to=os.devnull, stdout=None):
    if stdout is None:
        stdout = sys.stdout

    stdout_fd = fileno(stdout)
    # copy stdout_fd before it is overwritten
    #NOTE: `copied` is inheritable on Windows when duplicating a standard stream
    with os.fdopen(os.dup(stdout_fd), 'wb') as copied:
        stdout.flush()  # flush library buffers that dup2 knows nothing about
        try:
            os.dup2(fileno(to), stdout_fd)  # $ exec >&to
        except ValueError:  # filename
            with open(to, 'wb') as to_file:
                os.dup2(to_file.fileno(), stdout_fd)  # $ exec > to
        try:
            yield stdout # allow code to be run with the redirected stdout
        finally:
            # restore stdout to its previous value
            #NOTE: dup2 makes stdout_fd inheritable unconditionally
            stdout.flush()
            os.dup2(copied.fileno(), stdout_fd)

# here's the workflow we need. Should encapsulate this in a function.
myfile = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', delete=False)
with stdout_redirected(myfile):
        StandardTestLPs.test_lp_0(solver='CBC', duals=False, logLevel=1)
        sys.stdout.flush()
myfile.close()
myfileopened = open(myfile.name, 'r')
lines = myfileopened.readlines()
print(lines)

```

I think things will be even cleaner if we can create some kind of common module level apis for these Envs.
Something like the following:

- external_env.py
    - def is_env_type(env) -> bool: return isinstance(env, ExternalEnv)
    - def to_base_env(env, make_env, num_envs, ...) -> BaseEnv:
           ... logics for converting ExternalEnv to BaseEnv.
- multi_agent_env.py
    - def is_env_type(env) -> bool: return isinstance(env, MultiAgentEnv)
    - def to_base_env(env, make_env, num_envs, ...) -> BaseEnv:
           ... logics for converting MultiAgentEnv to BaseEnv.
- vector_env.py
    - def is_env_type(env) -> bool: return isinstance(env, VectorEnv)
    - def to_base_env(env, make_env, num_envs, ...) -> BaseEnv:
           ... logics for converting VectorEnv to BaseEnv.
- generic_env.py
    - def is_env_type(env) -> bool: return isinstance(env, EnvType)
    - def to_base_env(env, make_env, num_envs, ...) -> BaseEnv:
           ... logics for converting basically gym.Env to BaseEnv.
           ... basically the logics in the last big else clause.

Then with this structure, we can clean up this function to be as simple as:

```
def convert_to_base_env(env, ...) -> BaseEnv:
    if isinstance(env, BaseEnv):
        return env

    from ray.rllib.env import external_env, multi_agent_env, vector_env, generic_env
    for env_type in [external_env, multi_agent_env, vector_env, generic_env]:
        if env_type.is_env_type(env):
            return env_type.to_base_env(env, make_env, ...)

    raise ValueError("Unknown Env type: ", type(env))
```

Without this, `dataclasses.InitVar[<type>]` raises errors when returned by `typing.get_type_hints`:

```
>           raise TypeError(f"{msg} Got {arg!r:.100}.")
E           TypeError: Forward references must evaluate to types. Got dataclasses.InitVar[int].
```
The issue is, even if you were willing to rely on dataclasses to tell you what is or isn't an InitVar, you really need this function (or something similar) to allow you to resolve forward references to get what the generic parameter resolves to.

I think monkeypatching `InitVar` like this is preferable to copying the whole logic of `get_type_hints` with a tiny tweak to not error if the result isn't callable. But I suppose I could be convinced away from this if we think it's really problematic.

This change would break existing code that relies on this behavior _silently_. Code relying on this behavior might fail with a "file not found" error after `lightgbm::lightgbm()` has been called, resulting in possibly-significant time spent debugging to figure out that `{lightgbm}`'s default behavior has changed.

I agree with the argument in the pull request description that model `lightgbm::lightgbm()` shouldn't be responsible for persisting models to disk. I support just removing the argument `save_name` from `lightgbm()` entirely.  I think we should make that change right now, in this PR, without waiting for a round of deprecation warnings, because:

1.  it's already been almost 4 months since the most recent LightGBM release ([v3.3.1](https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/releases/tag/v3.3.1), excluding the special v3.3.2 that only had one small patch) and I expect it to be at least 2 months until the next, 4.0.0, release
2. this breaking change would result in a loud training-time error, which I consider much less expensive than than a silent breaking change or than one that happens at scoring time with a deployed model
